title: Defeat for Trump, victory for rule of law

audience_match: Filipino readers tracking the US 2020 election aftermath
Critics and supporters of Duterte's PH SC composition
Civic-minded readers concerned about presidential-judicial relations
Future Philippine and US presidents and their appointees

McConnell concession quoted: 'Many millions of us had hoped the presidential election would yield a different result. But our system of government has processes to determine who will be sworn in on January the 20th'
Estrada one-on-one meeting at Jose T. Pardo's house in Ayala Alabang (six years after Estrada's plunder conviction and pardon)
Estrada's question: 'During my two and half years in office, I named several justices to the Supreme Court. Why is it that none of them dissented from the 13-0 decision ousting me?'

Two conservative dissenters: Clarence Thomas, Samuel Alito

Jose T. Pardo (Estrada's former finance secretary; CJ's friend)

institutions: Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS)
cases: Estrada v. Desierto (March 2, 2001) Ã¢â‚¬â€� PH SC 13-0

stances.claim: SCOTUS's 7-2 rejection of Texas v. Pennsylvania Ã¢â‚¬â€� with all three Trump appointees (Kavanaugh, Gorsuch, Barrett) voting with the majority Ã¢â‚¬â€� was a 'victory for rule of law' that demonstrates 'despite Trump's occasional megalomania and authoritarian streak, the rule of law is much alive in the US,' and that the United States 'after losing its way during the last four years... could now be looked up to by the free world for leadership.'

Trump's remaining 'last card' Ã¢â‚¬â€� the Jan 6 congressional count Ã¢â‚¬â€� is 'really a joker' because Senate Republican leader Mitch McConnell has already conceded, acknowledging 'our system of government has processes to determine who will be sworn in on January the 20th.'

The SCOTUS rebuff parallels the Philippine Supreme Court's 13-0 decision in Estrada v. Desierto (March 2, 2001) ousting Estrada Ã¢â‚¬â€� and Estrada's later anguished question to CJ Panganiban ('Why is it that none of them dissented... If they could not side with me, why did they not just inhibit like you?') points to the central misunderstanding: judicial appointees owe no fealty to the appointing president.

The Constitution requires judges to be 'of proven competence, integrity, probity and independence'; those who don the Court's black robes 'are expected to undergo a transformation, to become different persons, to forfeit their past activities, relatives, and friends, especially the president who appointed them, and to devote their full time and attention to the exclusive and reclusive world of decision-making.'

The proper moral disposition of an appointing president is not disappointment but pride: 'presidents should assume a cutting of umbilical ties when jurists enter the hallowed doors of the Supreme Court. In fact, I believe, they should be happy, or at least consoled, that their appointees lived up to their sworn duty of Fiat justitia ruat caelum.'

notable_anecdotes.summary: The Estrada meeting six years after his conviction-and-pardon, arranged by Jose T. Pardo at Pardo's Ayala Alabang house Ã¢â‚¬â€� Estrada plaintively asking why none of his SCOTUS appointees dissented from the 13-0 decision, and 'If they could not side with me, why did they not just inhibit like you?' Ã¢â‚¬â€� preserved as the column's emotional centerpiece linking Manila to Washington.

The numerical-counterfactual analysis: had Kavanaugh, Gorsuch, and Barrett voted with Thomas and Alito, the result would have been 5-4 for Trump Ã¢â‚¬â€� a stark thought experiment showing how close the rule-of-law victory actually was.

CJ's coining of 'cutting of umbilical ties' as the proper presidential disposition toward judicial appointees Ã¢â‚¬â€� paired with the Latin sign-off 'Fiat justitia ruat caelum,' a CJ signature register marker.

deploys transformative-vocation doctrine to defeat appointing-president loyalty claims
treats appointing-president pride (not disappointment) as the correct normative disposition
frames 'rule of law' as the cross-cultural translation of judicial independence
applies black-robe transformation principle to anchor 'cutting of umbilical ties'

Critics and supporters of Duterte's PH SC composition

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban celebrates the US Supreme Court's 7-2 rejection of Texas v. Pennsylvania (Dec 11, 2020) Ã¢â‚¬â€� which had sought to subtract the 62 electoral votes of Georgia, Michigan, Pennsylvania, and Wisconsin from Biden's 306 and add them to Trump's 232 to flip the result to 294-244 by judicial fiat Ã¢â‚¬â€� and notes pointedly that all three Trump appointees (Brett Kavanaugh, Neil Gorsuch, and Amy Coney Barrett) voted with the majority, while only Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito dissented (and only to grant the motion to file, not on the merits). Had the three new appointees voted with their conservative seniors, the result would have been 5-4 for Trump. Trump's 'last card' at the January 6 congressional electoral count is 'really a joker' because Senate Republican leader Mitch McConnell has already conceded, acknowledging that 'our system of government has processes to determine who will be sworn in on January the 20th.' For CJ, the SCOTUS rebuff shows that 'despite Trump's occasional megalomania and authoritarian streak, the rule of law is much alive in the US' and that America, 'after losing its way during the last four years... could now be looked up to by the free world for leadership.' He draws the cross-jurisdictional parallel to Estrada v. Desierto (March 2, 2001), in which the Philippine SC unanimously (13-0, with CJ Davide and CJ Panganiban inhibiting) ousted Estrada in favor of VP Arroyo. Six years after his conviction-and-pardon, Estrada sought CJ out at Jose T. Pardo's Ayala Alabang house and asked why none of his own SC appointees dissented and 'If they could not side with me, why did they not just inhibit like you?' CJ's answer Ã¢â‚¬â€� preserved as the column's doctrinal core Ã¢â‚¬â€� is that judicial decisions 'must conform to the Constitution and the law, not to the wiles and whims of the governors,' and that those who 'don the Court's black robes are expected to undergo a transformation, to become different persons, to forfeit their past activities, relatives, and friends, especially the president who appointed them.' The moral lesson: 'presidents should assume a cutting of umbilical ties when jurists enter the hallowed doors of the Supreme Court. In fact, I believe, they should be happy, or at least consoled, that their appointees lived up to their sworn duty of Fiat justitia ruat caelum' Ã¢â‚¬â€� Let justice be done though the heavens may fall.
